title: Why the Schrodinger Equation Is a Theorem, Not a Postulate

metadata.color_law: TEAL = the derivation steps (probability conservation -> unitarity -> Hermitian generator -> exponential -> Schrodinger eq); CRIMSON = the postulate framing being displaced
metadata.note: Exclusions: no interaction-picture detour, no path-integral derivation, no Hamiltonian constraint in curved spacetime, no Stone's theorem

beats.narration_text: Every textbook presents the Schrodinger equation as a fundamental axiom — a thing we just accept because it works. One physical requirement makes it unavoidable.

card.title: Why the Schrodinger Equation Is a Theorem, Not a Postulate

beats.act: COLD OPEN — probabilities must sum to one
beats.narration_text: Start with one physical fact: the total probability of finding a particle somewhere must always equal one, no matter how the state changes in time. That is all you need.

beats.act: COLD OPEN — the constraint forces a form
beats.narration_text: That single requirement — probabilities sum to one — constrains time evolution to be a unitary operator. And from unitarity alone, step by step, the Schrodinger equation falls out.
If probabilities must always sum to one, what does that force time evolution to look like? Does it really force the exact equation textbooks call a postulate?

card.title: Does probability conservation force the Schrodinger equation?

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — postulate framing hides the chain
beats.narration_text: When you write the Schrodinger equation as a postulate, you hide a derivable chain. You could just as easily have postulated the wrong equation. The constraint actually picks out the right one.
Norm must be preserved at all times. The time-evolution operator U must satisfy U-dagger times U equals the identity. That is the definition of a unitary operator.

For an infinitesimal step, unitarity forces the generator to be Hermitian — that means its eigenvalues are real. Those eigenvalues are the energies. Real energies follow from probability conservation.
Compose N infinitesimal unitary steps. As N goes to infinity you get the matrix exponential: U of t equals e to the minus i H t over h-bar. This is the exact time-evolution operator.
Differentiate the exponential with respect to time. The result is exactly i h-bar times d-psi-d-t equals H-psi. The Schrodinger equation is the derivative of unitarity.

A corollary: because H must be Hermitian, its eigenvalues must be real. That means measured energies are always real numbers — not because we assumed it, but because probabilities must stay between zero and one.

A student is asked to derive time evolution from scratch, starting only from 'probabilities sum to one.' She writes U-dagger U equals I, extracts the Hermitian generator, composes N steps to get the exponential, and differentiates to find the Schrodinger equation — the equation she thought was given. She notes energies must be real, a fact she never questioned before.
Probability conservation forces unitarity. Unitarity forces a Hermitian generator. The exponential of a Hermitian generator, differentiated, is the Schrodinger equation. Not a postulate — a theorem.

card.title: Probability conservation forces the Schrodinger equation.

beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom series.
